title: Code of Conduct
text: shinytone project committed providing welcoming environment everyone. participants community, follow Contributor Covenant, version 2.1. contributing project (filing issues, opening pull requests, posting discussions), agree abide terms.

title: Code of Conduct
text: experience witness behaviour violates Code Conduct, please contact project maintainer: Chenzi Xu — chenzi.xu@ntu.edu.sg reports kept confidential.

title: Code of Conduct
text: maintainer review investigate every report respond manner deemed appropriate. Possible responses range private clarification permanent removal project’s communication channels.

title: Contributing to shinytone
text: Thanks interest helping improve shinytone! document outlines practical guidelines filing issues contributing code documentation.

title: Contributing to shinytone
text: best place report bugs suggest features GitHub issue tracker: https://github.com/chenchenzi/citationtone_hub/issues filing bug report, please include: short reproducible example, ideally using bundled data(sample_f0) dataset can run without data. output sessionInfo() (devtools::session_info()). exact error message behaviour saw, expected. feature requests, briefly describe use case (possible) feature fit existing API.

title: Contributing to shinytone
text: Small fixes (typos, doc clarifications, obvious bugs) welcome direct pull requests. larger changes, please open issue first discuss approach investing significant time. development workflow standard R-package one: Fork repo clone fork. Create feature branch main. Make changes. Run devtools::document() (regenerate man/) devtools::test() (run unit tests). Run devtools::check() aim 0 errors / 0 warnings. (One persistent NOTE “unused Imports” expected ignorable; relates bundled Shiny app.) Open pull request main clear description changed .

title: Contributing to shinytone
text: R code: follow tidyverse style guide. Function docs use roxygen2 markdown (Roxygen: list(markdown = TRUE) already set DESCRIPTION). Tests live tests/testthat/ use testthat 3 edition. Keep new exported functions consistent existing API: long-format data , named column arguments, defaults match bundled sample_f0 schema reasonable.

title: Contributing to shinytone
text: Shiny app lives inst/app/. UI module files organised inst/app/ui/. extending tab, please: Keep analytical logic function R/, inline observer, can tested independently. Use existing guess_var() helper smart selectInput defaults (see inst/app/global.R). Run app shinytone::run_app() confirm affected tab still works end end data(sample_f0).

title: Contributing to shinytone
text: Function documentation generated roxygen2 comments function R/. regenerate help files editing doc block, run devtools::document(). pkgdown docs site rebuilds automatically every push main via GitHub Actions. preview locally, run pkgdown::build_site().

title: Contributing to shinytone
text: Please note contributions subject Code Conduct.

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: shinytone research hub citation tone analysis tone languages. bundles: interactive Shiny app walks full workflow — pitch extraction, -speaker f0 normalisation, outlier detection, growth-curve generalised additive mixed models, Chao tone numeral summarisation. small set pure R functions implement analytical core, can run analyses scripted RMarkdown another package. vignette walks scripted side using package’s bundled sample dataset. prefer graphical workflow, run shinytone::run_app() installing, visit hosted app https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/.

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: examples assume three packages loaded:
code: library(shinytone) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: shinytone ships real citation-tone corpus available immediately library(shinytone). contains 38,808 f0 samples 1,848 tokens across 13 speakers. columns ’ll use: token — unique identifier recorded syllable (groups rows contour together) time — time seconds within token f0_Hz — fundamental frequency speaker — speaker ID tone — Chao tone category (1-5) char — Chinese character syllable See ?sample_f0 full schema source citation.

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: Raw Hz isn’t comparable across speakers (men women different baseline f0). normalise_f0() adds speaker_mean column plus either f0_st (semitones, default) f0_zscore, computed per speaker: Now f0_st puts every speaker comparable scale. plot mean contour per tone, use [compute_mean_contour()], normalises time [0, 1] within token averaging. package function returns result time / f0_predicted / tone schema used [predict_gca()] [predict_gamm()]:
code: normed <- normalise_f0(sample_f0,                        f0          = "f0_Hz",                        speaker     = "speaker",                        tone        = "tone",                        method      = "semitone",                        mean_method = "weighted") head(normed[, c("speaker", "tone", "f0_Hz", "speaker_mean", "f0_st")]) #>   speaker tone    f0_Hz speaker_mean     f0_st #> 1   dc102    3 245.7078     237.0675 0.6197450 #> 2   dc102    3 246.4516     237.0675 0.6720735 #> 3   dc102    3 247.5111     237.0675 0.7463436 #> 4   dc102    3 248.5496     237.0675 0.8188289 #> 5   dc102    3 249.7015     237.0675 0.8988754 #> 6   dc102    3 251.6647     237.0675 1.0344568 mean_contour <- compute_mean_contour(normed,                                      token = "token", f0 = "f0_st",                                      time  = "time",  tone = "tone")  ggplot(mean_contour, aes(time, f0_predicted, colour = factor(tone))) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   labs(x = "Normalised time", y = "f0 (semitones)", colour = "Tone")

what: Inspect for outliers and pitch-tracking artefacts
title: Getting started with shinytone
text: inspect_f0() flags two kinds issues: tokens whose per-token max min sits z_threshold SDs speaker’s mean, individual samples rate f0 change exceeds physiological plausibility (Sundberg 1973, Steffman & Cole 2022). flag_notes column gives human-readable reason flagged sample (e.g., "max high; jump (rise)"). live app, Inspect tab lets click flagged tokens one one.
code: inspected <- inspect_f0(sample_f0,                         f0      = "f0_Hz",                         token   = "token",                         time    = "time",                         speaker = "speaker",                         tone    = "tone")  # How many tokens were flagged overall? inspected |>   distinct(token, .keep_all = TRUE) |>   count(flagged_token) #> # A tibble: 2 × 2 #>   flagged_token     n #>   <lgl>         <int> #> 1 FALSE          1667 #> 2 TRUE            181

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: fit_polynomial() returns one row per token Legendre polynomial coefficients (c0, c1, c2, …). Useful features downstream classifiers, compact summary contour shape. Interpretation: c0 ≈ token-level mean f0 (semitones, relative speaker mean) c1 ≈ linear slope across contour (positive = rising) c2 ≈ curvature (positive = U-shaped, negative = inverted-U)
code: poly_coefs <- fit_polynomial(normed,                              f0      = "f0_st",                              token   = "token",                              time    = "time",                              speaker = "speaker",                              tone    = "tone",                              degree  = 2) head(poly_coefs) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   token            speaker  tone    c0    c1    c2 #>   <chr>            <chr>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 dc102一1s22.3525 dc102       6 -1.53  3.86 0.114 #> 2 dc102一2s22.9425 dc102       6 -1.07  2.62 0.282 #> 3 dc102一3s23.5725 dc102       6 -1.37  2.79 0.474 #> 4 dc102一4s24.1425 dc102       6 -1.14  1.76 0.732 #> 5 dc102一5s24.7925 dc102       6 -1.09  1.90 0.296 #> 6 dc102一6s25.3125 dc102       6 -1.87  2.14 1.28

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: fit_gca() convenience wrapper around lme4::lmer() sensible defaults tone-shape modelling — orthogonal polynomials per-token normalised time, plus conventional random effects speaker item. non-standard model structures, call lme4::lmer() directly.
code: # (Skipped on package build for speed; uncomment to run locally.) gca <- fit_gca(normed,                f0      = "f0_st",                time    = "time",                token   = "token",                tone    = "tone",                speaker = "speaker",                item    = "char",                 # use Chinese char as item                degree  = 2,                random_slope_speaker = FALSE,                random_slope_item    = FALSE)  # Population-level per-tone curves preds <- predict_gca(gca, n = 100) ggplot(preds, aes(time, f0_predicted, colour = tone)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1)

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: contour_to_chao() reduces per-tone mean (model-predicted) contours Chao numerals — 5-level digit strings like 55, 35, 214. Three conversion methods one pass: refline — reference-line , rounded [1, 5] scale interval — interval-based , ceiling (0, 5] scale robust — robust using μ ± σ highest- lowest-tone extremes (requires raw_data) shape column summarises contour (“rising”, “falling”, “dipping”, etc.) derived reference-line numeral via classify_contour().
code: # Pre-aggregate into a per-tone mean contour over all speakers mean_contour <- compute_mean_contour(sample_f0,                                      token = "token", f0 = "f0_Hz",                                      time  = "time",  tone = "tone")  chao <- contour_to_chao(   mean_contour,   raw_data  = sample_f0,   raw_token = "token", raw_f0 = "f0_Hz", raw_tone = "tone" ) chao[, c("tone", "refline", "interval", "robust", "shape")] #>   tone refline interval robust         shape #> 1    1      22       22     33 mid-low level #> 2    2     212      111    222       dipping #> 3    3      32       32     33       falling #> 4    4      45       45     44        rising #> 5    5      22       21     32 mid-low level #> 6    6      23       13     23        rising

what: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: Citation Tone Research Hub: interactive Shiny app R package citation-tone research workflows across tone languages. Developed Chenzi Xu. Co-authored Cong Zhang.

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: Overview Features Two ways use Installation Quick start online app enough Documentation Citation License Authors

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: shinytone integrates full citation-tone analysis workflow single place: pitch extraction, -speaker f0 normalisation, growth-curve generalised additive mixed-effects models, outlier artefact inspection, Chao tone numeral summarisation. designed phoneticians, typologists, fieldworkers, students working lexical tone production.

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: 🎙️ F0 processing: extract f0 .wav files wrassp Praat, including downloadable Praat script tailored settings. 🔍 Inspect: token-level outlier flags (-speaker z-score) sample-level artefact detection (octave jumps, rate--change violations, carryover frames; Steffman & Cole 2022). 📏 Normalise: -speaker semitone z-score normalisation, simple weighted speaker means. 📈 Visualise: f0 contours coloured tone, optional faceting speaker. 📊 Model: per-token Legendre polynomials, growth-curve analysis (GCA) orthogonal polynomials, generalised additive mixed models (GAMM) optional AR1 correction. 🧮 Summarise: convert contours Chao tone numerals using three methods (reference-line, interval, robust).

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: hosted version runs https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/. Best classroom demos, trying workflow without setup, analysis per-upload batches stay ~100 MB.

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: either launch Shiny UI offline: …call analytical functions directly script RMarkdown document:
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("chenchenzi/citationtone_hub") shinytone::run_app() library(shinytone) data(sample_f0)  # Normalise to semitones, by speaker normed <- normalise_f0(sample_f0,                        f0      = "f0_Hz",                        speaker = "speaker",                        tone    = "tone",                        method  = "semitone")  # Convert mean contours to Chao tone numerals mc   <- compute_mean_contour(sample_f0,                              token = "token", f0 = "f0_Hz",                              time  = "time",  tone = "tone") chao <- contour_to_chao(mc,                         raw_data  = sample_f0,                         raw_token = "token",                         raw_f0    = "f0_Hz",                         raw_tone  = "tone") chao[, c("tone", "refline", "interval", "robust", "shape")]

what: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: Returns short human-readable shape name (e.g., "level", "rising", "falling", "dipping", "peaking", one finer-grained "low level" / "mid level" / "high level" labels repeated-digit strings). Used contour_to_chao() add shape column output, also exposed standalone utility classifying numerals external sources.

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
code: classify_contour(chao_str)

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: chao_str character string Chao digits, e.g. "55", "214", "35".

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: character string naming shape.

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: Classification rules: digits identical: one "low level", "mid-low level", "mid level", "mid-high level", "high level" according digit value. Three digits interior minimum endpoints: "dipping". Three digits interior maximum endpoints: "peaking". Otherwise, "rising" last digit higher first, "falling" lower, "level" equal.

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: Chao, Y. R. (1930). system tone-letters. Le Maître Phonétique, 30, 24–27. Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
code: classify_contour("55")    # "high level" #> [1] "high level" classify_contour("214")   # "dipping" #> [1] "dipping" classify_contour("35")    # "rising" #> [1] "rising" classify_contour("51")    # "falling" #> [1] "falling"

what: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
text: Aggregates long-format f0 dataset per-tone mean contour suitable plotting, comparison, Chao numeral summarisation via contour_to_chao(). function used internally Summarise tab Shiny app user chooses raw normalised dataset input.

title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
code: compute_mean_contour(   data,   token = "token",   f0 = "f0",   time = "time",   tone = "tone",   n_bins = 50 )

title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
text: data long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. token, f0, time, tone Column names. n_bins Number evenly-spaced time bins across [0, 1]. Default 50. Larger values give smoother contour cost noisier per-bin estimates bins sparsely populated.

title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
text: data frame columns tone, time, f0_predicted, one row per (tone, time-bin).

title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
text: Internally: Within token, normalise time axis [0, 1] tokens different durations can averaged across grid. Round sample's normalised time onto one n_bins equally-spaced bins. (tone, time-bin) cell, compute mean f0 across tokens tone samples bin. Return long-format mean contour columns tone, time, f0_predicted (column name f0_predicted matches convention used predict_gca() predict_gamm() downstream tools can consume either model predictions raw mean contours).

title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
text: Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour

what: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: Summarises per-tone contours Chao tone numerals (Chao 1930): 5-level digit strings like "55", "35", "214" describe contour endpoints interior turning point. Three conversion methods computed one pass analyst can compare pick whichever appropriate corpus (Xu & Zhang 2024).

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
code: contour_to_chao(   contour_data,   tone_col = "tone",   time_col = "time",   f0_col = "f0_predicted",   threshold = 0.5,   raw_data = NULL,   raw_token = "token",   raw_f0 = "f0",   raw_tone = "tone" )

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: contour_data Per-tone contour data, typically compute_mean_contour(), predict_gca(), predict_gamm(). Must contain tone, time, f0_predicted columns (rename via tone_col, time_col, f0_col arguments). tone_col, time_col, f0_col Column names within contour_data. threshold Chao-units threshold turning-point detection. Default 0.5. See Details. raw_data Optional long-format raw data, used compute robust . NULL, robust column left NA. raw_token, raw_f0, raw_tone Column names raw_data.

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: data frame one row per tone, containing: tone: tone label. n_digits: number digits numeral (2 3). refline: reference-line method numeral, e.g. "55". interval: interval-based numeral, e.g. "45". robust: robust numeral (NA raw_data given). shape: shape name classify_contour() applied refline. sample_times, ref_continuous, int_continuous, rob_continuous: list columns per-sample numeric values underlying digit strings, useful plotting analysis. Two attributes attached returned data frame: f0_min f0_max input contour, plus robust_stats list containing means standard deviations used robust method (NULL raw_data supplied).

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: three use Fraction Range () normalisation, maps f0 onto bounded scale representing speaker's (corpus's) pitch range. differ range defined scaled values mapped Chao digits. Reference-line [1, 5]: f0' = (f0 - f0_min) / (f0_max - f0_min) * 4 + 1, rounded. Five reference lines integer values; sampled point snaps nearest integer. Interval-based (0, 5]: f0' = (f0 - f0_min) / (f0_max - f0_min) * 5, ceilinged. Five equal-width intervals; sampled point assigned interval via ceiling(). Robust (0, 5] (raw_data supplied): interval formula, range defined μ ± σ per-token f0 peaks highest-pitched tone (max) per-token f0 valleys lowest-pitched tone (min), following Zhu (1999) recommendation Xu & Zhang (2024). robust outliers using corpus-wide min max.

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: tone, function samples contour either two endpoints (yielding 2-digit numeral) two endpoints plus one interior turning point (yielding 3-digit numeral). 3-digit case applies either: interior minimum threshold Chao units endpoints (dip), interior maximum threshold Chao units endpoints (peak). default threshold = 0.5 (half Chao unit [1, 5] scale) conservative choice. Lower thresholds produce 3-digit numerals, useful tonally rich languages.

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: Chao, Y. R. (1930). system tone-letters. Le Maître Phonétique, 30, 24–27. Zhu, X. (1999). Shanghai Tonetics. LINCOM Europa. Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
code: data(sample_f0) mc <- compute_mean_contour(sample_f0,                            token = "token", f0 = "f0_Hz",                            time  = "time",  tone = "tone") chao <- contour_to_chao(mc,                         raw_data  = sample_f0,                         raw_token = "token",                         raw_f0    = "f0_Hz",                         raw_tone  = "tone") chao[, c("tone", "refline", "interval", "robust", "shape")] #>   tone refline interval robust         shape #> 1    1      22       22     33 mid-low level #> 2    2     212      111    222       dipping #> 3    3      32       32     33       falling #> 4    4      45       45     44        rising #> 5    5      22       21     32 mid-low level #> 6    6      23       13     23        rising

what: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: Fits Generalised Additive Mixed Model smooth (non-polynomial) tone--time interactions. GAMMs increasingly common alternative GCA dynamic speech analysis capture arbitrary smooth contour shapes without requiring analyst choose polynomial degree (Wood 2017; Sóskuthy 2021; Xu & Zhang 2024). convenience wrapper around mgcv::bam() prepares data, builds smooth-based formula optional difference smooths speaker random smooths, fits model, optionally refits AR1 correlation. non-standard model structures, call mgcv::bam() mgcv::gam() directly.

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
code: fit_gamm(   data,   f0 = "f0",   time = "time",   token = "token",   tone = "tone",   speaker = "speaker",   item = "item",   duration = NULL,   k = 10,   bs = "tp",   smooth_type = c("separate", "difference"),   random_intercept_speaker = TRUE,   random_intercept_item = TRUE,   random_smooth = c("none", "speaker", "speaker_tone", "speaker_by_tone", "ref_diff"),   use_ar1 = FALSE )

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: data long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. f0, time, token, tone, speaker, item Column names. duration Optional column name per-sample duration covariate. Use NULL omit duration smooth. k Basis dimension time smooth. Default 10. bs Spline basis type passed mgcv::s(). Default "tp" (thin-plate regression spline). smooth_type Either "separate" (one smooth per tone via s(time, = tone)) "difference" (reference smooth plus difference smooths via ordered factor). random_intercept_speaker, random_intercept_item Logical; include corresponding s(..., bs = "re") random intercept. random_smooth One "none", "speaker", "speaker_tone", "speaker_by_tone", "ref_diff". See Details. use_ar1 Logical. TRUE, fit , estimate rho residuals' lag-1 autocorrelation, refit rho.

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: S3 object class "shinytone_gamm", list : model: fitted mgcv::bam() object. formula_str: user-readable formula string. rho: NULL AR1 rho value. convergence_warning: NULL captured warning message. smooth_type, random_smooth: passed (needed predict_gamm()). col_names: original column names, back-mapping.

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: Normalise time [0, 1] per token. Coerce tone, speaker, item, (difference smooths) ordered tone_ord factors treatment contrasts. Build formula based smooth_type random_smooth. Fit mgcv::bam() using discrete = TRUE speed large datasets, capturing warnings. use_ar1 = TRUE, estimate rho lag-1 autocorrelation residuals refit rho plus per-token AR.start correct within-token correlation.

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: "separate" (default) fits one smooth per tone s(time, = tone). tone curve estimated independently. "difference" fits reference smooth plus difference smooths via ordered factor. Lets test whether non-reference tone significantly differs reference point along time axis.

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: Following Sóskuthy (2021), several patterns supported via random_smooth: "speaker": single -speaker factor smooth s(time, speaker, bs = "fs", m = 1). Fastest, basic random-curve modelling. "speaker_tone": interaction factor smooth s(time, speaker.tone, bs = "fs", m = 1) letting speaker-tone combination random curve. "speaker_by_tone": -speaker factor smooths separately per tone. "ref_diff": reference -speaker smooth plus tone-difference random smooths, matched smooth_type = "difference". "none": random-effect intercepts () included. Refer Sóskuthy (2021) guidance choosing .

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: Sóskuthy, M. (2021). Evaluating generalised additive mixed modelling strategies dynamic speech analysis. Journal Phonetics, 84, 101017. doi:10.1016/j.wocn.2020.101017 Wood, S. N. (2017). Generalized Additive Models: Introduction R (2nd ed.). Chapman Hall/CRC. Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(sample_f0) normed <- normalise_f0(sample_f0,                        f0      = "f0_Hz",                        speaker = "speaker",                        tone    = "tone") gamm <- fit_gamm(normed,                  f0          = "f0_st",                  time        = "time",                  token       = "token",                  tone        = "tone",                  speaker     = "speaker",                  item        = "char",                  k           = 10,                  smooth_type = "separate",                  random_smooth = "speaker",                  use_ar1     = TRUE) predict_gamm(gamm, n = 200) } # }

what: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: Fits Growth Curve Analysis model f0 contours: linear mixed-effects regression f0 orthogonal polynomial terms time, interacted tone, conventional -speaker -item random effects. GCA standard analysis framework time-varying dynamic speech data (Mirman 2014; Xu & Zhang 2024) recommended approach want statistical inference per-tone shape parameters intercept, slope, curvature. convenience wrapper around lme4::lmer() prepares data way citation-tone use case typically wants. non-standard model structures (custom contrasts, additional fixed effects, alternative random-effects structures), call lme4::lmer() directly.

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
code: fit_gca(   data,   f0 = "f0",   time = "time",   token = "token",   tone = "tone",   speaker = "speaker",   item = "item",   degree = 2,   random_intercept_speaker = TRUE,   random_intercept_item = TRUE,   random_slope_speaker = TRUE,   random_slope_item = FALSE )

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: data long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. f0, time, token, tone, speaker, item Column names f0, time within token, token ID, tone category, speaker ID, item word. degree Polynomial degree, one 1, 2, 3. Default 2. random_intercept_speaker, random_intercept_item Logical; include -speaker -item random intercept. Default TRUE. random_slope_speaker, random_slope_item Logical; include -speaker -item random slopes orthogonal-polynomial terms (ot1 ... otK). Default TRUE speaker, FALSE item.

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: S3 object class "shinytone_gca", list : model: fitted lme4::lmerMod object. formula_str: user-readable formula string (original column names). poly_coefs: coefs stats::poly() needed predict_gca(). degree: polynomial degree. col_names: original column names (back-mapping summary tables). convergence_warning: NULL captured warning message.

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: Within token, normalise time axis [0, 1]. Build orthogonal polynomial terms ot1, ot2, ..., otK normalised time using stats::poly(), caching basis coefficients predict_gca() can reuse . Construct lmer formula form f0 ~ (ot1 + ... + otK) * tone + (random effects) using options decide random effects include. Fit model REML, capturing convergence warning caller can decide whether act . Return everything bundled S3 object class "shinytone_gca", downstream helpers (predict_gca(), summary(), etc.) need.

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: degree = 1: linear (intercept + slope). Sufficient purely rising falling contours. degree = 2 (default): linear + quadratic. Captures rising/falling/dipping/peaking shapes. usual choice. degree = 3: adds cubic term sigmoid S-shaped contours. Useful languages elaborate contour shapes. Higher degrees increase model complexity risk -fitting; also slow fit can degrade convergence.

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: defaults follow conventional GCA practice citation-tone work: -speaker random intercepts slopes polynomial terms, plus -item random intercept. Add -item slopes items vary systematically shape, drop random slopes convergence difficult.

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca

Journal Statistical Software, 67(1), 1–48. doi:10.18637/jss.v067.i01 Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca

what: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: token, normalises time axis [-1, 1], constructs Legendre polynomial basis requested degree, fits ordinary least squares. Returns one row per token fitted coefficients c0, c1, ..., c{degree}. Useful compact, interpretable summary contour shape, features downstream classifiers regression models (Mirman 2014; Xu & Zhang 2024).

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
code: fit_polynomial(   data,   f0 = "f0",   token = "token",   time = "time",   speaker = "speaker",   tone = "tone",   degree = 2 )

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: data long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. f0 Column name f0 values. Default "f0". Using normalised f0 (e.g. "f0_st" normalise_f0()) recommended cross-speaker comparison. token Column name token ID. Default "token". time Column name time within token. Default "time". speaker Column name speaker ID, carried output. Default "speaker". tone Column name tone category, carried output. Default "tone". degree Polynomial degree. One 1, 2, 3. Default 2.

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: token-level data frame one row per token, containing token, speaker, tone, coefficient columns c0, c1, ..., c{degree}.

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: token: Compute per-token range time rescale linearly interval [-1, 1], Legendre polynomials orthogonal. Build Legendre basis requested degree. Solve ordinary least-squares problem stats::lm.fit() recover coefficients.

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: Legendre polynomials orthogonal [-1, 1], fitted coefficients tidy shape interpretation: c0: token-level mean f0. c1: linear slope across contour. Positive means rising. c2: quadratic curvature. Positive means U-shaped (dipping), negative means inverted-U (peaking). c3: cubic shape. Differentiates sigmoid reverse-sigmoid contours.

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: Tokens fewer degree + 1 valid samples receive NA coefficients. Tokens single unique time point keep c0 mean f0 set higher-order coefficients NA.

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: Mirman, D. (2014). Growth Curve Analysis Visualization Using R. Chapman Hall/CRC. Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
code: data(sample_f0) normed <- normalise_f0(sample_f0,                        f0      = "f0_Hz",                        speaker = "speaker",                        tone    = "tone") coefs <- fit_polynomial(normed,                         f0      = "f0_st",                         token   = "token",                         time    = "time",                         speaker = "speaker",                         tone    = "tone",                         degree  = 2) head(coefs) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   token            speaker  tone    c0    c1    c2 #>   <chr>            <chr>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 dc102一1s22.3525 dc102       6 -1.53  3.86 0.114 #> 2 dc102一2s22.9425 dc102       6 -1.07  2.62 0.282 #> 3 dc102一3s23.5725 dc102       6 -1.37  2.79 0.474 #> 4 dc102一4s24.1425 dc102       6 -1.14  1.76 0.732 #> 5 dc102一5s24.7925 dc102       6 -1.09  1.90 0.296 #> 6 dc102一6s25.3125 dc102       6 -1.87  2.14 1.28

what: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
text: Identifies tokens whose maximum minimum f0 lies far centre speaker's per-token f0 distribution. Suitable first pass detecting tracking errors, mis-segmentations, genuinely unusual productions fitting contour models. Token-level outlier removal recommended cleaning step prior citation-tone analysis (Xu & Zhang 2024).

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
code: flag_outliers(   data,   f0 = "f0",   token = "token",   speaker = "speaker",   z_threshold = 3 )

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
text: data long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. f0 Column name f0 Hz. Default "f0". token Column name token ID. Default "token". speaker Column name speaker ID. Default "speaker". z_threshold Absolute z-score token flagged. Default 3, covering 99.7% normal distribution.

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
text: token-level data frame (one row per token) columns: f0_token_max, f0_token_min, f0_token_mean, f0_token_sd, z_max, z_min, flag_too_high, flag_too_low, plus original token speaker columns.

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
text: Drop samples f0 NA 0 (pitch trackers commonly output 0 unvoiced frames). Compute per-token maximum, minimum, mean, SD f0. Within speaker, z-score per-token maxima per-token minima (speaker consistently high f0 flagged outlier corpus). Flag token |z_max| > z_threshold (per-token max high low speaker) |z_min| > z_threshold.

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
text: default 3 corresponds standard convention ±3 SDs covers 99.7% normal distribution, assumption 0.3% tokens flagged. Lower thresholds (e.g., 2 2.5) aggressive, useful manual review every flagged token feasible. Higher thresholds (4+) conservative. Token z-scoring per speaker assumes speaker contributes enough tokens SD meaningfully estimated; results speakers handful tokens treated advisory.

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
text: Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
code: data(sample_f0) out <- flag_outliers(sample_f0,                      f0      = "f0_Hz",                      token   = "token",                      speaker = "speaker",                      z_threshold = 3) table(out$flag_too_high, useNA = "ifany") #>  #> FALSE  TRUE  #>  1839     9  table(out$flag_too_low,  useNA = "ifany") #>  #> FALSE  TRUE  #>  1845     3

what: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: Detects sample-level pitch-tracking artefacts inside token. Three classes artefact flagged: rate--change violations (faster rises falls human vocal folds can plausibly produce), octave jumps (pitch halving doubling), carryover samples follow flagged frame stay close enough error suspected part artefact. Used sample-level counterpart flag_outliers() Inspect tab Shiny app inside inspect_f0().

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
code: flag_pitch_jumps(   data,   f0 = "f0",   token = "token",   time = "time",   time_unit = c("s", "ms", "norm"),   rise_threshold = 1.263,   fall_threshold = 1.714,   octave_bounds = c(0.49, 1.99),   carryover_mult = 1.5 )

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: data long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. f0 Column name f0 Hz. Default "f0". token Column name token ID. Default "token". time Column name time. Default "time". time_unit One "s", "ms", "norm". Default "s". "norm" option treats step one 10 ms-equivalent unit. rise_threshold Maximum plausible rise ST per 10 ms. Default 1.263 (Sundberg 1973). fall_threshold Maximum plausible fall ST per 10 ms. Default 1.714 (Sundberg 1973). octave_bounds Hz-ratio bounds outside step flagged octave jump. Default c(0.49, 1.99) (halving doubling). carryover_mult Carryover band multiple rise/fall threshold (semitones). 0 disables carryover. Default 1.5.

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: input data frame two appended columns: flagged_jump: logical, TRUE samples flagged artefacts carryover frames. jump_note: character describing flag (e.g., "jump (rise)", "jump (fall)", "octave jump", "carryover", compound notes joined "; ").

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: Treat f0 = 0 NA (pitch trackers commonly mark unvoiced frames 0 Hz). Within token, compute semitone difference consecutive f0 samples Hz ratio . Rescale observed rate change semitones-per-10-ms equivalent using time_unit, result can compared fixed threshold (Sundberg 1973). Flag landing sample (.e., sample immediately jump) octave jumps, threshold-violating rises, threshold-violating falls. Extend flagged sample forward chain carryover frames stay within carryover_mult * threshold semitones error sample's f0 (Steffman & Cole 2022).

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: Default rise_threshold fall_threshold come Sundberg's (1973) classic study maximum rate f0 change achievable singing. Changes exceeding rates physiologically implausible almost always tracking errors. Tightening values catches borderline cases cost false positives. Default octave_bounds = c(0.49, 1.99) flags successive Hz ratio extreme approximate halving doubling, dominant failure mode autocorrelation-based pitch trackers. Setting carryover_mult = 0 disables carryover extension flags landing sample jump. default 1.5 follows Steffman & Cole (2022).

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: Steffman, J., & Cole, J. (2022). Pitch tracking artefacts detection voicing errors spontaneous speech. Sundberg, J. (1973). acoustics singing voice. Scientific American, 229(3), 82–91. Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

what: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
text: One-call convenience wrapper runs flag_outliers() flag_pitch_jumps() dataset, joins results back one long-format data frame, produces single flag_notes column human-readable reasons flag. function backs Inspect tab Shiny app.

title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
code: inspect_f0(   data,   f0 = "f0",   token = "token",   time = "time",   speaker = "speaker",   tone = "tone",   z_threshold = 3,   rise_threshold = 1.263,   fall_threshold = 1.714,   octave_bounds = c(0.49, 1.99),   carryover_mult = 1.5,   time_unit = c("s", "ms", "norm") )

title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
text: data long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. f0 Column name f0 Hz. Default "f0". token Column name token ID. Default "token". time Column name time. Default "time". speaker Column name speaker ID. Default "speaker". tone Column name tone category. Default "tone". z_threshold Absolute z-score token flagged. Default 3, covering 99.7% normal distribution. rise_threshold Maximum plausible rise ST per 10 ms. Default 1.263 (Sundberg 1973). fall_threshold Maximum plausible fall ST per 10 ms. Default 1.714 (Sundberg 1973). octave_bounds Hz-ratio bounds outside step flagged octave jump. Default c(0.49, 1.99) (halving doubling). carryover_mult Carryover band multiple rise/fall threshold (semitones). 0 disables carryover. Default 1.5. time_unit One "s", "ms", "norm". Default "s". "norm" option treats step one 10 ms-equivalent unit.

title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
text: long-format data frame containing original token, time, f0, speaker, tone columns plus: f0_token_max, f0_token_min, f0_token_mean, f0_token_sd: per-token summary statistics. flagged_jump: logical, sample-level jump flag. flagged_token: logical, TRUE token extreme value sample-level jump. flag_notes: human-readable concatenation reasons sample flagged.

title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
text: Call flag_outliers() get token-level outlier flags (flag_too_high, flag_too_low) plus per-token summary statistics. Call flag_pitch_jumps() get sample-level pitch-tracking artefact flags (flagged_jump, jump_note). Left-join token-level flags onto long-format jump output, every sample carries kinds information. Set flagged_token TRUE token max/min z-outlier contains least one sample-level jump. Concatenate human-readable reasons single flag_notes column display. Use individual functions (flag_outliers(), flag_pitch_jumps()) want one kind check want combine outputs non-standard way.

title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
text: Steffman, J., & Cole, J. (2022). Pitch tracking artefacts detection voicing errors spontaneous speech. Sundberg, J. (1973). acoustics singing voice. Scientific American, 229(3), 82–91. Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
code: data(sample_f0) result <- inspect_f0(sample_f0,                      f0      = "f0_Hz",                      token   = "token",                      time    = "time",                      speaker = "speaker",                      tone    = "tone")  # How many tokens were flagged at default thresholds? table(unique(result[, c("token", "flagged_token")])$flagged_token) #>  #> FALSE  TRUE  #>  1667   181   # Inspect the first few flagged samples head(result[result$flagged_jump, c("token", "time", "f0_Hz", "flag_notes")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 4 #>   token             time f0_Hz flag_notes  #>   <chr>            <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       #> 1 dc102地4s46.6425 0.37   286. jump (rise) #> 2 dc103笛4s50.6725 0.126  197. jump (fall) #> 3 dc103笛4s50.6725 0.143  193. carryover   #> 4 dc103笛4s50.6725 0.160  194. carryover   #> 5 dc103笛4s50.6725 0.176  195. carryover   #> 6 dc103笛4s50.6725 0.192  195. carryover

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: Adds within-speaker normalised f0 column long-format f0 data frame. Speaker normalisation puts contours male female speakers comparable scale, near-universal pre-processing step cross-speaker tone modelling, plotting, contour summarisation (Rose 1987; Zhu 1999). Two formulae available. Semitones speaker's mean give perceptually uniform scale. -speaker z-scores combine centring unit-variance scaling. formulae rely per-speaker mean f0, can computed two ways: simple arithmetic mean observations, mean per-tone means every tone contributes equally speaker's centre.

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
code: normalise_f0(   data,   f0 = "f0",   speaker = "speaker",   tone = "tone",   method = c("semitone", "zscore"),   mean_method = c("weighted", "simple") )

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: data long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. Must contain least f0, speaker, ("weighted" means) tone columns named . f0 Name column raw f0 values, Hz. Default "f0". speaker Name speaker ID column. Default "speaker". tone Name tone category column. Default "tone". read mean_method = "weighted". method Normalisation formula. One : "semitone" (default): ST = 12 * log2(f0 / speaker_mean). Result 0 speaker mean ±12 one octave . "zscore": Z = (f0 - speaker_mean) / sd(speaker_f0). Result mean approximately 0 SD approximately 1 within speaker. mean_method per-speaker mean used method computed: "weighted" (default; mean per-tone means) "simple" (arithmetic mean across observations).

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: input data frame two added columns: speaker_mean: per-speaker mean used normalisation. f0_st (method = "semitone") f0_zscore (method = "zscore").

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: Compute per-speaker mean f0 using mean_method (see ). Join mean back data new column called speaker_mean. Apply selected normalisation formula sample sample return augmented data frame.

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: "semitone" standard citation-tone work. scale perceptually uniform (one semitone roughly one just-noticeable pitch step), centred speaker's range, preserves logarithmic structure musical pitch perception. "zscore" useful centring scaling desired, .e., want remove speaker's baseline equalise f0 variability. Useful cross-speaker statistical models raw variance differs strongly across speakers.

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: "weighted" (default) first computes mean f0 per (speaker, tone) cell, averages per-tone means. tone contributes equally speaker's centre regardless many tokens . right default unbalanced designs token counts differ across tones, common fieldwork naturalistic corpora. "simple" takes arithmetic mean every f0 observation speaker. Faster, equivalent weighted design balanced.

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: summary statistics use na.rm = TRUE. Sample-level NA f0 values propagate NA normalised column.

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: Rose, P. (1987). Considerations normalisation fundamental frequency linguistic tone. Speech Communication, 6(4), 343–352. doi:10.1016/0167-6393(87)90008-1 Zhu, X. (1999). Shanghai Tonetics. LINCOM Europa. Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
code: data(sample_f0)  out <- normalise_f0(sample_f0,                     f0      = "f0_Hz",                     speaker = "speaker",                     tone    = "tone",                     method  = "semitone") head(out[, c("speaker", "tone", "f0_Hz", "speaker_mean", "f0_st")]) #>   speaker tone    f0_Hz speaker_mean     f0_st #> 1   dc102    3 245.7078     237.0675 0.6197450 #> 2   dc102    3 246.4516     237.0675 0.6720735 #> 3   dc102    3 247.5111     237.0675 0.7463436 #> 4   dc102    3 248.5496     237.0675 0.8188289 #> 5   dc102    3 249.7015     237.0675 0.8988754 #> 6   dc102    3 251.6647     237.0675 1.0344568  # Same data, z-score instead of semitones: out_z <- normalise_f0(sample_f0,                       f0      = "f0_Hz",                       speaker = "speaker",                       tone    = "tone",                       method  = "zscore") head(out_z[, c("speaker", "f0_Hz", "speaker_mean", "f0_zscore")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 4 #>   speaker f0_Hz speaker_mean f0_zscore #>   <chr>   <dbl>        <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 dc102    246.         237.     0.194 #> 2 dc102    246.         237.     0.210 #> 3 dc102    248.         237.     0.234 #> 4 dc102    249.         237.     0.257 #> 5 dc102    250.         237.     0.283 #> 6 dc102    252.         237.     0.327

title: Predict population-level f0 smooth curves from a GAMM fit — predict_gamm

Set random-effect columns (speaker, item, random-smooth grouping factors) first level respective factors; reference values placeholders affect prediction corresponding terms excluded. Identify random-effect terms need excluded prediction reflects population-average fixed smooths. Call stats::predict() mgcv model exclude = <random terms> se.fit = TRUE also return standard errors. Predictions scale f0 column used fit model (typically semitones passed f0 = "f0_st" normalise_f0()).

what: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
text: Boots bundled Shiny application ships inside installed package. Equivalent hosted version https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/ running machine, file-size, memory, session limits audio / CSV files never leave computer.

title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
code: run_app(...)

title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
text: ... Additional arguments forwarded shiny::runApp() — e.g. port = 4321, launch.browser = FALSE, host = "0.0.0.0".

title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
text: return; runs app stopped.

title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   shinytone::run_app()   shinytone::run_app(port = 4321, launch.browser = FALSE) } # }

title: Sample f0 contour dataset — sample_f0
text: subset citation-tone f0 recordings, used running example across vignettes, function @examples blocks, Shiny app's "Try sample data" button. Available library(shinytone) thanks LazyData: true DESCRIPTION.

title: Sample f0 contour dataset — sample_f0
code: sample_f0

title: Sample f0 contour dataset — sample_f0
text: data frame 38,808 rows 12 columns: token Unique identifier recorded token / syllable. Rows belonging contour share token. index Sample index within token, starting 1. time Time seconds within token. f0_Hz Fundamental frequency Hz. intensity Sample intensity (dB). speaker Speaker ID (13 speakers total). char Chinese character corresponding syllable. position Position syllable source utterance. start_time Start time token within source recording. tone Tone category (integer codes 1-6 dataset). ipa IPA transcription syllable's vowel. vowel Coarse vowel category.

title: Sample f0 contour dataset — sample_f0
text: Xu, C. (2025). Plastic Mandarin tones: regional identity prosody. Phonetica, 82(5), 331–362. doi:10.1515/phon-2025-0001

title: Sample f0 contour dataset — sample_f0

what: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
title: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
text: interactive Shiny application accompanying R functions citation tone research across tone languages. Integrates full workflow pitch extraction, -speaker f0 normalisation, growth-curve generalised additive mixed modelling, outlier inspection, Chao tone numeral summarisation.

title: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
text: hosted version app available https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/. local launcher (run_app()) added future release UI can served offline package installed.

title: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
text: Standalone analytical functions (normalise_f0(), fit_gca(), fit_gamm(), contour_to_chao(), ...) extracted Shiny app stages. See NEWS.md current state.

title: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
text: Maintainer: Chenzi Xu chenzi.xu@ntu.edu.sg Authors: Chenzi Xu chenzi.xu@ntu.edu.sg Cong Zhang

title: shinytone (development version)
text: First public release R package, alongside existing online Shiny app https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/. Package skin stage: Shiny app continues run unchanged. Standalone analytical functions (normalise_f0(), fit_gca(), fit_gamm(), contour_to_chao(), …) extracted subsequent releases.
